title.emphasis: Introduction: The Illusion of the Neutral Platform

section.para: Social media platforms like Meta (Facebook) and X (formerly Twitter) present themselves as neutral conduits for user expression, governed by a simple bargain: in exchange for access to a "free" service, users grant the platform a license to host their content. However, a deeper legal analysis reveals a far more complex and directive relationship. The platform's architecture—from its User Experience (UX) design to its extensive content moderation policies—functions as a powerful mechanism of external control, shaping and directing the creation of user-generated intellectual property (IP).

This report argues that the relationship between a social media platform and its content-creating user is best understood as a unilateral contract for services. The platform makes a standing, public offer, and the user accepts not with a promise, but by performing the specific act of creating and uploading content that conforms to the platform's meticulously defined and ever-changing rules. This act of creation is not one of pure, unfettered expression; it is a service rendered to the platform, which in turn monetizes the resulting IP.
